title: Domaine de Bon Augure, an exciting wine estate in the Languedoc with very interesting soils
summary: Website: https://www.domainedebonaugure.fr/ Cédric Guy farms 9 hectares of very interesting vineyards near the village of Joncels, with distinctive soils. They
content:
Website: https://www.domainedebonaugure.fr/

Cédric Guy farms 9 hectares of very interesting vineyards near the village of Joncels, with distinctive soils. They are ‘breche calcaire’, which translates as brecchiated limestone. Small limestone pebbles are cemented together, and this is really distinctive.

The wines are currently labelled IGP Haute Vallée de l’Orb, but soon they may be part of a new AOP called Monts d’Avene, which will be restricted to Chardonnay on the brecchiated limestone. Bon Augure has 6 hectares of Chardonnay, and also some Petit Manseng, Petite Arvine (sur echalas), Sauvignon Gris, Grenache Gris and Pinot Gris.

Farming is organic/biodynamic, and the altitude here is 600 m.

These wines are imported into the UK by Saison Wines.

Domaine de Bon Augure En Terre Étrangère 2024  IGP Haute Vallée de l’Orb, France
13% alcohol. Chardonnay, 50% barrel fermented, used oak. Textured, fresh and linear with lovely weight and real precision. There’s concentration and depth, but it’s not in the least bit fat, with citrus and pear fruit, finishing fresh and focused. 94/100 (UK retail c. £20)

Domaine de Bon Augure Joncs-Cella 2024 IGP Haute Vallée de l’Orb, France
13% alcohol. The name is Latin for Joncels. This is 80% Chardonnay and 20% Petit Manseng, fully barrel fermented in new oak. This is very fine and textural with a spicy framing to the rich pear and citrus fruit. Lovely precision with a fine acid line. Real focus and depth here, and it stays fresh with a nice bitter hint on the finish. 94/100 (UK retail c. £24)

Domaine de Bon Augure Aux Innocents Les Mains Pleines 2024 IGP Haute Vallée de l’Orb, France
This translates to ‘to the innocents, full hands’. It’s a skin-fermented wine with Sauvignon Gris, Grenache Gris and Pinot Gris, all of which have some pigmentation in their skins, so not technically an orange wine. 7 days on skins in stainless steel. Vivid amber colour. Refined, concentrated, powerful. Lovely intensity here with bold fruit and a slight saltiness on the finish. Great acidity, and real sense of refinement. 95/100 (UK retail c. £20)

Domaine de Bon Augure DSLS 2021 IGP Haute Vallée de l’Orb, France
13% alcohol. Petite Arvine that spends 2 years in new barrels, which are from the ‘nuits’ range of Sirugue, a higher quality barrel designed for the most concentrated wines. And it soaks up the oak very well. Amazing vivid spicy nose with crystalline citrus fruit. The palate has spice, citrus and nuts with

title: Highlights: Tenuta San Guido La Difese 2024 Toscana IGT, Italy
summary: This is the bargain in the line-up from Tenuta San Guido: of course, Sassicaia and Guidalberto are amazing, but they
content:
This is the bargain in the line-up from Tenuta San Guido: of course, Sassicaia and Guidalberto are amazing, but they aren’t that accessible, whereas this delivers a lot of the personality at a more affordable price. I really like it.

Tenuta San Guido La Difese 2024 Toscana IGT, Italy
13.5% alcohol. 75% Sangiovese and 25% Cabernet Sauvignon. This is a beautiful expression of Bolgheri that’s bright, linear, juicy and tart with sour cherry and raspberry, as well as amazing presence. There are some nice green hints along with the juicy fruits, and good acidity. A wholesome, fresh, linear wine with good tannins and acidity. Such elegance and drinkability. 93/100 (UK retail is c. £30)

title: Prestige Provence rosés and whites from Château Sainte Marguerite
summary: Website: https://www.chateausaintemarguerite.com/en/ This was a masterclass at Wine Paris, focusing on this important estate from Provence. It was presented by
content:
Website: https://www.chateausaintemarguerite.com/en/

This was a masterclass at Wine Paris, focusing on this important estate from Provence. It was presented by Raimonds Tomsons, the well known Latvian sommelier, and Olivier Fayard, the CEO and chief winemaker at Sainte Marguerite.

Olivier’s parents Brigitte and Jean-Pierre Fayard bought the domaine in 1977. It has 450 hectares of vineyards in two locations that are both classified as Cru Classé.

It was back in 1955 that 23 estates became Cru Classé in Provence. Eighteen of these remain, and are able to use this label. Historically, the classification was based on examining terroirs, and out of 70 estates considered, only 23 were awarded the badge.

The first Marguerite terroir is La Londe, which is close to the sea, and has mica schist soils that are compact and stony. It gets a lot of wind.

The second terroir is further in land, called Pierrefeu, which has limestone clay soils, mild winters and lower rainfall, with significant sunshine hours.

In terms of the plant material, they have been working with famous pepeneriste Lilian Berrillon, and have a massale selection of Grenache and Cinsault, plus Vermentino from Corsica.

Four years ago Marguerite went into a partnership with Pernod Ricard. These wines are among the top tier of Provence in terms of pricing, but they also deliver.

Château Sainte Marguerite Symphonie Rosé 2025 Cru Classé Côtes de Provence, France
13% alcohol. This was first made in 1999. It’s 50% Grenache, 45% Cinsault and 5% Vermentino (Rolle). They use the cuvée and the pressings for this. Fruity aromatics and a lovely texture on the palate. Has brightness, juicy citrus and some cherry and pear. Pure and youthful with a slight chalkiness, finishing with grapefruit and lemon. 91/100

Château Sainte Marguerite Fantastique Rosé 2025 Cru Classé Côtes de Provence, France
13% alcohol. First made in 2017, this is 45% each of Grenache and Cinsault and 10% Rolle. Just cuvée, not pressings. Subtle, pure and fine with lovely grapefruit notes as wll as supple cherry and cranberry characters. Fresh and linear with a nice chalky undercurrent. Subtle peach notes, too. There’s a real elegance to this wine. 93/100

Château Sainte Marguerite Marguerites en Provence Rosé 2025 Cru Classé Côtes de Provence, France
13% alcohol. Just from La Londe, and its mica schist soils. 60% Grenache, 20% Cinsault and 20% Rolle. This is fresh and elegant. Bright with delicate mandarin, pear and grapefruit characters. There’s a lovely mid-palate texture to this wine, which is fine and expressive with nice texture on the mid-palate. Some salinity on the finish. 93/100

They have been making white wines since the 1980s, largely from seaside plots such as Le Désirade.

Château Sainte Marguerite Symphonie Blanc 2025 Cru Classé Côtes de Provence, France
This is Rolle, 70% from La Londe and 20% from Pierrefeu. Stainless steel only. Lively and fresh but also textural, with nice acidity but also a saline twist and some intriguing notes of wild herbs, pear, peach and lime. Compact now, but will expand given a bit of time. Preserved lemons and almond on the finish. 93/100

Château Sainte Marguerite Fantastique Blanc 2024 Cru Classé Côtes de Provence, France
13.5% alcohol. Rolle, Lalonde only, barrel fermented with 50% new oak. Very stylish with smoky grapefruit, some peach and pear fruit, and also some oak that adds a nice spicy framing. Fresh and textural and quite refined. 93/100

title: Prophet’s Rock in Central Otago is one of New Zealand’s top wineries, excelling with Pinot Noir, Riesling and Pinot Gris
summary: Website: https://www.prophetsrock.co.nz/ At wine Paris I caught up with Paul Pujol (pictured above) to taste through the new releases from
content:
Website: https://www.prophetsrock.co.nz/

At wine Paris I caught up with Paul Pujol (pictured above) to taste through the new releases from Prophet’s Rock, one of the leading wineries in Central Otago, New Zealand.

I’ve written extensively about Prophet’s Rock here. Paul has an interesting background, and spent three years as chief winemaker at Kuentz Bas in Alsace before returning New Zealand to head up Prophet’s Rock. He’s taken this winery to new heights, and it’s in the peer group with the region’s best. Notable is the emphasis on Riesling: these are ageworthy and delicious, as is the Pinot Gris. But Pinot Noir is the star turn, and there are now a range of premium bottlings, including the François Millet collaboration.

Prophet’s Rock Retrospect White 2024 Central Otago, New Zealand
This is a blend of four Alsace grape varieties fermented together. Juicy, lively and expressive with great balance. Mineral with fine spices and some wet stone detail, showing such complexity and balance. 95/100

Prophet’s Rock Dry Riesling 2023 Central Otago, New Zealand
From a single parcel with schist soils. Bright and expressive with a mineral streak and lovely balance. Textured citrus fruit, showing real drive but also generosity. 94/100

Prophet’s Rock Dry Riesling 2016 Central Otago, New Zealand
Lively, vivid and bright with appealing citrus fruit and a stony, mineral edge. Very stylish, with nice weight. 94/100

Prophet’s Rock Pinot Gris 2024 Central Otago, New Zealand
Complex and layered, this is dry but has some richness. Lovely depth and minerality, showing good weight in the mouth and finishes savoury. 93/100

Prophet’s Rock Pinot Gris 2010 Central Otago, New Zealand
Stony and mineral with wet stone and apple notes showing lovely depth. This is very stylish and is ageing beautifully. 93/100

Prophet’s Rock Antipodes Blanc 2022 Central Otago, New Zealand
This is the Chardonnay collaboration with François Millet of Comte de Vogüe in Burgundy. Complex, mineral and layered with freshness. Stony and vivid with lovely citrus fruit core. Very expressive. 95/100

Prophets Rock Pinot Noir Sophora 2023 Central Otago, New Zealand
This is from the valley floor on fine schist gravels. Juicy, stony and mineral with bright fruit and nice acidity. A multidimensional wine that’s stony and mineral and shows traces of iodine and blood savouriness. 94/100

Prophet’s Rock Pinot Noir Olearia 2023 Central Otago, New Zealand
Schist bedrock with some clay. Concentrated, stony and mineral. This shows real elegance to the cherry and berry fruit. Good structure. Such finesse here. 95/100

Prophet’s Rock Pinot Noir Home Vineyard 2023 Central Otago, New Zealand
Juicy and textural with ripe cherry and strawberry fruit, with a slight saline edge. Nice brightness and focus, but has a rich mid-palate. 95/100

Prophet’s Rock Antipodes 2022 Central Otago, New Zealand
The original collaboration wine with François Millet. Pinot Noir, made his way. So textural and fine with nice precision to the fruit and a saline twist on the finish. Nice density and structure with a purity and elegance, too. This is brilliant. 95/100

Prophet’s Rock Retrospect 2019 Central Otago, New Zealand
Nice depth here with a stony edge and some iodine notes. Grippy and textural with layers of flavour. Stony and mineral, this is developing nicely. 95/100

title: Smart organic wines from A&D Wines, Vinho Verde, Portugal
summary: Website: https://andwines.pt/en/ This is a project based in the Baião subregion of Vinho Verde right next to the Douro. It
content:
Website: https://andwines.pt/en/

This is a project based in the Baião subregion of Vinho Verde right next to the Douro. It began with a family quinta Casa do Arrabalde (5 hectares of vines), and another property Quinta dos Espinhosos (7 hectares of vines) and this was then supplemented with the purchase of the much larger (33 hectares) Quinta de Santa Teresa.

They now have 45 hectares of vines all organically certified. Oenotourism is important here. All ferments are with indigenous yeasts.

Casa do Arrabalde 2024 Vinho Verde, Portugal
A field blend of Avesso, Alvarinho and Arinto from vineyards at 550 m. Fresh and supple with nice weight, and a strong mineral edge to the textured citrus fruit. Lively. 92/100

Quinta de Santa Teresa Monólogo Santa Teresa Avesso 2024 Vinho Verde, Portugal
Fresh, bright and linear with lovely weight and texture. Has direct citrus and pear fruit, and a nice crystalline quality. Very stylish. 93/100

Quinta de Santa Teresa Monólogo Santa Teresa Arinto 2024 Vinho Verde, Portugal
Fresh, bright and quite linear with lovely purity. Slightly salty lemony directness but also some nice texture. Lovely wine. 93/100

Quinta de Santa Teresa Singular 2024 Vinho Verde, Portugal
This is a blend of Avesso, Arinto, Alvarinho and Malvasia Fina. 10% in oak. So textural and lively with bright lemony fruit and some ripe pear. Fresh and linear with a nice acid line. Pure with a wide dynamic range. 93/100

Quinta de Santa Teresa Avesso 2024 Vinho Verde, Portugal
Nice density with bright, pure citrus fruit. Good concentration, showing mineral-laced citrus and nice saline detail. Really expressive. 94/100

Quinta de Santa Teresa Esculpido Avesso Vinhas Velhas 2023 Vinho Verde, Portugal
13% alcohol. Old vines, 20% new oak. Powerful and layered with some spicy oak supporting the lively lemony fruit. Has freshness and concentration, with the oak adding an extra dimension. 92/100

Quinta de Santa Teresa Rosé 2024 Vinho Verde, Portugal
This is 90% Touriga Nacional, 10% Vinhão. Textured and layered with sappy red cherry fruit and some strawberry. Bright but with good texture. Stylish. 92/100

Quinta de Santa Teresa Avesso Curtimenta 2023 Vinho Verde, Portugal
Fermented on skins for two weeks. Bight, lively and expressive with citrus, peach and some zippy acidity. Good structure and nice brightness, showing lovely energy. 93/100

UK agent: Graft Wines

title: The wines of Son Antem Alaró, Mallorca, Spain (updated with new releases)
summary: Website: https://www.sonantemalaro.es/en/ Mallorca isn’t well known as a wine island, but there are some interesting projects here, and a recent
content:
Website: https://www.sonantemalaro.es/en/

Mallorca isn’t well known as a wine island, but there are some interesting projects here, and a recent addition to the fine wine scene here is the Son Antem project, which was started by a wine-importing couple in Sweden, Johan and Marin Lidby. I tried through the range and came away impressed.

Son Antem Alaró has 18 hectares of vines, all hand managed by the Lidby family.

Son Antem Malva 2024 Vino de la Tierra Mallorca, Spain
14% alcohol. This white is powerful, rich and intense with a lively acid line lying under the rich pear and peach fruit. The limestone gives the bracing acidity that provides a nice foil for the bold, slightly pithy fruit. Such nice intensity to this distinctive white wine, with a lively acid character driving things. 93/100

Son Antem Aura 2024 Vino de la Tierra Mallorca, Spain
13% alcohol. This white is made from the Giró Rus variety. Bold, rich and textural with some honey, nut and spice characters as well as some pear and peach. There’s innate richness, but it stays fresh and crystalline, with a touch of aniseed and some grainy detail. 92/100

Camins de Son Antem Callet 2024 Vino de la Tierra Mallorca, Spain
12.5% alcohol. This is a lighter-coloured red wine with beautiful aromatics of sweet strawberries and some glace cherry. In the mouth it is very textural with white pepper, cherry, strawberry and fine spicy notes. Lovely fine-grained texture here. Sleek and sweetly fruited with finesse. 94/100

Son Antem S’Era 2024 Vino de la Tierra Mallorca, Spain
12.5% alcohol. Relatively pale in colour, this is is juicy, fine and delicate. There’s a rich mid-palate core of sweet cherry and plum fruit with some damson bitterness, and also some dried herb character. The variety here is Callet, and it seems brilliantly adapted to this warm climate, with some rose, cherry, aniseed and mediterranean herb notes. It’s a little compressed on the finish, with some tannin and acid providing structure, but overall this is really compelling. 94/100

Camins de Son Antem Negre 2024 Vino de la Tierra Mallorca, Spain
14% alcohol. Manto Negro, Monastrell and Syrah. This is sleek, ripe and textured with appealing black cherry and berry fruits. There’s an innate richness and ripeness, but it’s hemmed in by a bit of grainy structure and just enough acidity. And the fruit character stays deliciously floral and fresh. 93/100

Older notes:

Son Antem Camins de Mallorca Giro Blanco 2023 Mallorca, Spain
12.5% alcohol. This is concentrated and grainy and salty with sweet, textured, ripe pear and citrus fruit (a touch of mandarin and lemon). This has such a lovely mineral flow on the palate with real finesse. Very expressive: ripe but well defined. 94/100

Son Antem Malva 2023 Mallorca
14% alcohol. This is really rich and expansive with sweet pear and table grape fruitiness, as well as some spicy highlights and a touch of saltiness. Rich, exuberant and really fruity, this has lots of personality, and good acidity balancing the sweet fruit. 91/100

Son Antem Camins de Mallorca Rouge 2022 Mallorca, Spain
12.5% alcohol. Lovely aromatics here with sleek cherries, raspberries and some aniseed, as well as a hint of pepper. The palate has a sweet fruit core with nice spicy detail. Pure and refined with warm Mediterranean herb notes but always so balanced with real finesse. Grainy and delicious. 93/100

Son Antem Alaró S’Era 2023 IGP Mallorca, Spain
12% alcohol. This is varietal Callet. Quite a pale cherry red colour, in the in-between land, not quite rosé, not quite red wine. Very fine and supple with sweet red cherry fruit and a silky elegance, and just a hint of structure. Has lovely texture and warmth with rounded sweet cherry fruit. 94/100

Son Antem Vespre 2022 Mallorca, Spain
12.5% alcohol. This is supple, expressive and elegant with real elegance, and a sweet, textured mid-palate with stony cherries and plums, and then notes of herbs, pepper and fine spices framing the fruit on the finish. This is very fine: restrained, sappy and showing warm-climate elegance. 94/100

Son Antem Sa Tanca 2022 Mallorca, Spain
13% alcohol. This is Manto Negro, from iron-rich stony soils. Beautiful aromatics: sweet cherry and raspberry fruit with some nice spicy detail and a beautiful flow across the palate. Ripe but really elegance with great purity. 95/100

title: Highlights: Louis Jadot Famille Gagey Bouzeron 2023
summary: Website: https://www.louisjadot.com/bo/wp-content/pdf/vins/vin-1325-bouzeron.pdf Previously labelled as Domaine Gagey, this is now Famille Gagey, presumably because some non-domaine fruit enters the blend.
content:
Website: https://www.louisjadot.com/bo/wp-content/pdf/vins/vin-1325-bouzeron.pdf

Previously labelled as Domaine Gagey, this is now Famille Gagey, presumably because some non-domaine fruit enters the blend. Bouzeron is a small appellation in Bourgogne of 47 hectares, devoted solely to Aligoté. This is a superb example of what this variety can do, and it’s vinified two-thirds in tank and then one-third in barrels, of which 20% are new.

Louis Jadot Famille Gagey Bouzeron 2023 Bourgogne, France
This is a really good Aligoté. Lovely aromatics with lively citrus notes as well as ripe pear. The palate is fine grained and textural with lovely depth of flavour, and notes of pear skin, crystalline lemons and a hint of apricot. So lovely, showing real depth, and a slight saltiness on the finish. 94/100

title: How pruning is done in Bourgogne (Burgundy)
summary: I was recently filming in Bourgogne (Burgundy), and there were lots of gaps while cameras and lighting were being set
content:
I was recently filming in Bourgogne (Burgundy), and there were lots of gaps while cameras and lighting were being set up. So I went to look at the vines. It was early April and the buds had burst and the leaves were coming out. It’s a good time to see the way the vines are pruned. Here are a bunch of pictures showing some of the variation, almost all taken in Grand Cru Corton Charlemagne vineyards.

Viticulture is quite uniform here. Vines are planted at high density, with 10 000 of them per hectare, and 1 x 1 m spacing. The fruiting wire is low at around 30 cm. The vines are single-Guyot pruned, with a short cane of 6-8 buds, and a two bud replacement spur if there’s one to be found. Each vine is only being asked to ripen a small quantity of grapes because of the close spacing. Canopies aren’t high and because of the spacing, over-row tractors are used, stradding the row they are treating.

I saw very little herbicide use: these are expensive vineyards, and almost everyone works the soil. In less fancy vineyards, the situation might be different. Once you leave the more celebrated appellations vineyards can look quite different in terms of spacing and pruning (for example, the Hautes Côtes).

title: Invincible, a new Douro star winery from Marc Kent and Rita Marques
summary: Website: https://invincible.com.pt/ This is the Douro project of Marc Kent and Rita Marques. Marc is well known as the founder
content:
Website: https://invincible.com.pt/

This is the Douro project of Marc Kent and Rita Marques. Marc is well known as the founder and head of Boekenhoutskloof in South Africa, but he’s fallen in love with the Douro and now spends most of his time there. Rita previously made wines under the Conceito label, including a fabulous Bastardo that I was in love with!

Invincible (full name Companhia de Vinhos Invencível) began in 2000, so it’s still early days for this project, and the top wines are only being released now. The winery is based in the Cima Corgo, right in the heart of the Douro.

These wines range from everyday affordable (the Numero Um is around £12 in the UK, and is available in supermarkets) to more ambitious. They also make a Vinho Verde called Natural Mystic, which retails for £16.

Invincible Número Um Branco 2025 Douro, Portugal (sample)
A little bit of Muscat is used here, fermented in amphora. The resy of the wine is in concrete. Bright and stony with lovely pure fruit. Crisp with nice balance, and real potential. 91/100

Invincible Número Dois Branco 2024 Douro, Portugal
Made from older vineyards, this is half concrete and half barrel. Some oak adds an extra dimension. Bright and linear, this is fresh with some nutty, mealy hints, with nice balance. 91/100

Invincible ‘The Invincible’ Branco 2024 Douro, Portugal
This is first release. Juicy and bright, this is a vivid white wine with nice intensity. Zippy with some stony minerality and a touch of spicy oak. Very sophisticated. 93/100

Invincible Número Um Tinto 2024 Douro, Portugal
Juicy, bright and fruity with a nice focus to the sweet raspberry and cherry fruit. Vital. Lovely fruit with liqourice and a touch of pepper. 92/100 (this is £12.75 in Sainsbury’s in the UK.)

Invincible Número Dois Tinto 2023 Douro, Portugal
60 year old vines, fermented in concrete tanks, then spends one year in barrel and another year in concrete tank. Juicy, bright and vivid with nice spicy framing to the fresh cherry and berry fruits, with a lick of liquorice. Modern in style showing bright fruit, with some tarriness around the margins. 92/100

Invincible ‘The Invincible’ Tinto 2023 Douro, Portugal
From a vineyard planted in 1930. Concentrated but supple with lovely pure black cherry and blackberry fruit with a nice salty edge and some good tannic structure. Very fine and expressive. 94/100

Natural Mystic Vinhas Velhas 2025 Vinho Verde, Portugal
Mostly Loureiro. Lively, vivid and bright with nice focus and fruitiness. Very zippy but has texture and balance. 92/100

title: Brindiwell, a new producer in Devon, England
summary: Website: https://www.brindiwell.co.uk/pages/about-us I first met James Matyear when he was managing the vineyard at Black Chalk. But with his wife
content:
Website: https://www.brindiwell.co.uk/pages/about-us

I first met James Matyear when he was managing the vineyard at Black Chalk. But with his wife Jo he’s now got his own project. Back in 2016 they found their site, and after diligent preparation, planted in 2018. Viticulture is organic less the certification, with no herbicides used. They’ve also planted 1000 native trees on the site, including 600 oak trees. This is very cool.

Brindiwell Blanc de Noirs 2022 Devon, England
12.2% alcohol. 26 months on lees, with 9% of the base wine aged in barrel. This is fresh and aromatic with lovely pure cherry and lemon fruit, showing nice delicacy and texture. Fruit-driven with real finesse and purity, and a hint of sherbetty brightness on the finish. The balance here is superb: it’s fruit dominant, but not techno, with some personality. This is the combination of a really good site, good viticulture and sensitive winemaking. And it’s just the first release. 92/100

Brindiwell Pinot Noir Rosé 2023 Devon, England
12.6% alcohol. Clones 114, 115 and 777, stainless steel ferment and ageing, 25% malolactic fermentation. Pale in colour, this is a really fine, delicate, textural rose with sweet red cherry fruit, a touch of redcurrant and raspberry, with some fine sappy, leafy hints in the background. There’s a nice sleek quality to the fruit, and it finishes with a fine crystalline spicy quality. This is really good. 92/100

title: Domaine de Bon Augure, an exciting wine estate in the Languedoc with very interesting soils
summary: Website: https://www.domainedebonaugure.fr/ Cédric Guy farms 9 hectares of very interesting vineyards near the village of Joncels, with distinctive soils. They
content:
Website: https://www.domainedebonaugure.fr/

Cédric Guy farms 9 hectares of very interesting vineyards near the village of Joncels, with distinctive soils. They are ‘breche calcaire’, which translates as brecchiated limestone. Small limestone pebbles are cemented together, and this is really distinctive.

The wines are currently labelled IGP Haute Vallée de l’Orb, but soon they may be part of a new AOP called Monts d’Avene, which will be restricted to Chardonnay on the brecchiated limestone. Bon Augure has 6 hectares of Chardonnay, and also some Petit Manseng, Petite Arvine (sur echalas), Sauvignon Gris, Grenache Gris and Pinot Gris.

Farming is organic/biodynamic, and the altitude here is 600 m.

These wines are imported into the UK by Saison Wines.

Domaine de Bon Augure En Terre Étrangère 2024  IGP Haute Vallée de l’Orb, France
13% alcohol. Chardonnay, 50% barrel fermented, used oak. Textured, fresh and linear with lovely weight and real precision. There’s concentration and depth, but it’s not in the least bit fat, with citrus and pear fruit, finishing fresh and focused. 94/100 (UK retail c. £20)

Domaine de Bon Augure Joncs-Cella 2024 IGP Haute Vallée de l’Orb, France
13% alcohol. The name is Latin for Joncels. This is 80% Chardonnay and 20% Petit Manseng, fully barrel fermented in new oak. This is very fine and textural with a spicy framing to the rich pear and citrus fruit. Lovely precision with a fine acid line. Real focus and depth here, and it stays fresh with a nice bitter hint on the finish. 94/100 (UK retail c. £24)

Domaine de Bon Augure Aux Innocents Les Mains Pleines 2024 IGP Haute Vallée de l’Orb, France
This translates to ‘to the innocents, full hands’. It’s a skin-fermented wine with Sauvignon Gris, Grenache Gris and Pinot Gris, all of which have some pigmentation in their skins, so not technically an orange wine. 7 days on skins in stainless steel. Vivid amber colour. Refined, concentrated, powerful. Lovely intensity here with bold fruit and a slight saltiness on the finish. Great acidity, and real sense of refinement. 95/100 (UK retail c. £20)

Domaine de Bon Augure DSLS 2021 IGP Haute Vallée de l’Orb, France
13% alcohol. Petite Arvine that spends 2 years in new barrels, which are from the ‘nuits’ range of Sirugue, a higher quality barrel designed for the most concentrated wines. And it soaks up the oak very well. Amazing vivid spicy nose with crystalline citrus fruit. The palate has spice, citrus and nuts with

title: Highlights: Tenuta San Guido La Difese 2024 Toscana IGT, Italy
summary: This is the bargain in the line-up from Tenuta San Guido: of course, Sassicaia and Guidalberto are amazing, but they
content:
This is the bargain in the line-up from Tenuta San Guido: of course, Sassicaia and Guidalberto are amazing, but they aren’t that accessible, whereas this delivers a lot of the personality at a more affordable price. I really like it.

Tenuta San Guido La Difese 2024 Toscana IGT, Italy
13.5% alcohol. 75% Sangiovese and 25% Cabernet Sauvignon. This is a beautiful expression of Bolgheri that’s bright, linear, juicy and tart with sour cherry and raspberry, as well as amazing presence. There are some nice green hints along with the juicy fruits, and good acidity. A wholesome, fresh, linear wine with good tannins and acidity. Such elegance and drinkability. 93/100 (UK retail is c. £30)

title: Prestige Provence rosés and whites from Château Sainte Marguerite
summary: Website: https://www.chateausaintemarguerite.com/en/ This was a masterclass at Wine Paris, focusing on this important estate from Provence. It was presented by
content:
Website: https://www.chateausaintemarguerite.com/en/

This was a masterclass at Wine Paris, focusing on this important estate from Provence. It was presented by Raimonds Tomsons, the well known Latvian sommelier, and Olivier Fayard, the CEO and chief winemaker at Sainte Marguerite.

Olivier’s parents Brigitte and Jean-Pierre Fayard bought the domaine in 1977. It has 450 hectares of vineyards in two locations that are both classified as Cru Classé.

It was back in 1955 that 23 estates became Cru Classé in Provence. Eighteen of these remain, and are able to use this label. Historically, the classification was based on examining terroirs, and out of 70 estates considered, only 23 were awarded the badge.

The first Marguerite terroir is La Londe, which is close to the sea, and has mica schist soils that are compact and stony. It gets a lot of wind.

The second terroir is further in land, called Pierrefeu, which has limestone clay soils, mild winters and lower rainfall, with significant sunshine hours.

In terms of the plant material, they have been working with famous pepeneriste Lilian Berrillon, and have a massale selection of Grenache and Cinsault, plus Vermentino from Corsica.

Four years ago Marguerite went into a partnership with Pernod Ricard. These wines are among the top tier of Provence in terms of pricing, but they also deliver.

Château Sainte Marguerite Symphonie Rosé 2025 Cru Classé Côtes de Provence, France
13% alcohol. This was first made in 1999. It’s 50% Grenache, 45% Cinsault and 5% Vermentino (Rolle). They use the cuvée and the pressings for this. Fruity aromatics and a lovely texture on the palate. Has brightness, juicy citrus and some cherry and pear. Pure and youthful with a slight chalkiness, finishing with grapefruit and lemon. 91/100

Château Sainte Marguerite Fantastique Rosé 2025 Cru Classé Côtes de Provence, France
13% alcohol. First made in 2017, this is 45% each of Grenache and Cinsault and 10% Rolle. Just cuvée, not pressings. Subtle, pure and fine with lovely grapefruit notes as wll as supple cherry and cranberry characters. Fresh and linear with a nice chalky undercurrent. Subtle peach notes, too. There’s a real elegance to this wine. 93/100

Château Sainte Marguerite Marguerites en Provence Rosé 2025 Cru Classé Côtes de Provence, France
13% alcohol. Just from La Londe, and its mica schist soils. 60% Grenache, 20% Cinsault and 20% Rolle. This is fresh and elegant. Bright with delicate mandarin, pear and grapefruit characters. There’s a lovely mid-palate texture to this wine, which is fine and expressive with nice texture on the mid-palate. Some salinity on the finish. 93/100

They have been making white wines since the 1980s, largely from seaside plots such as Le Désirade.

Château Sainte Marguerite Symphonie Blanc 2025 Cru Classé Côtes de Provence, France
This is Rolle, 70% from La Londe and 20% from Pierrefeu. Stainless steel only. Lively and fresh but also textural, with nice acidity but also a saline twist and some intriguing notes of wild herbs, pear, peach and lime. Compact now, but will expand given a bit of time. Preserved lemons and almond on the finish. 93/100

Château Sainte Marguerite Fantastique Blanc 2024 Cru Classé Côtes de Provence, France
13.5% alcohol. Rolle, Lalonde only, barrel fermented with 50% new oak. Very stylish with smoky grapefruit, some peach and pear fruit, and also some oak that adds a nice spicy framing. Fresh and textural and quite refined. 93/100

title: Prophet’s Rock in Central Otago is one of New Zealand’s top wineries, excelling with Pinot Noir, Riesling and Pinot Gris
summary: Website: https://www.prophetsrock.co.nz/ At wine Paris I caught up with Paul Pujol (pictured above) to taste through the new releases from
content:
Website: https://www.prophetsrock.co.nz/

At wine Paris I caught up with Paul Pujol (pictured above) to taste through the new releases from Prophet’s Rock, one of the leading wineries in Central Otago, New Zealand.

I’ve written extensively about Prophet’s Rock here. Paul has an interesting background, and spent three years as chief winemaker at Kuentz Bas in Alsace before returning New Zealand to head up Prophet’s Rock. He’s taken this winery to new heights, and it’s in the peer group with the region’s best. Notable is the emphasis on Riesling: these are ageworthy and delicious, as is the Pinot Gris. But Pinot Noir is the star turn, and there are now a range of premium bottlings, including the François Millet collaboration.

Prophet’s Rock Retrospect White 2024 Central Otago, New Zealand
This is a blend of four Alsace grape varieties fermented together. Juicy, lively and expressive with great balance. Mineral with fine spices and some wet stone detail, showing such complexity and balance. 95/100

Prophet’s Rock Dry Riesling 2023 Central Otago, New Zealand
From a single parcel with schist soils. Bright and expressive with a mineral streak and lovely balance. Textured citrus fruit, showing real drive but also generosity. 94/100

Prophet’s Rock Dry Riesling 2016 Central Otago, New Zealand
Lively, vivid and bright with appealing citrus fruit and a stony, mineral edge. Very stylish, with nice weight. 94/100

Prophet’s Rock Pinot Gris 2024 Central Otago, New Zealand
Complex and layered, this is dry but has some richness. Lovely depth and minerality, showing good weight in the mouth and finishes savoury. 93/100

Prophet’s Rock Pinot Gris 2010 Central Otago, New Zealand
Stony and mineral with wet stone and apple notes showing lovely depth. This is very stylish and is ageing beautifully. 93/100

Prophet’s Rock Antipodes Blanc 2022 Central Otago, New Zealand
This is the Chardonnay collaboration with François Millet of Comte de Vogüe in Burgundy. Complex, mineral and layered with freshness. Stony and vivid with lovely citrus fruit core. Very expressive. 95/100

Prophets Rock Pinot Noir Sophora 2023 Central Otago, New Zealand
This is from the valley floor on fine schist gravels. Juicy, stony and mineral with bright fruit and nice acidity. A multidimensional wine that’s stony and mineral and shows traces of iodine and blood savouriness. 94/100

Prophet’s Rock Pinot Noir Olearia 2023 Central Otago, New Zealand
Schist bedrock with some clay. Concentrated, stony and mineral. This shows real elegance to the cherry and berry fruit. Good structure. Such finesse here. 95/100

Prophet’s Rock Pinot Noir Home Vineyard 2023 Central Otago, New Zealand
Juicy and textural with ripe cherry and strawberry fruit, with a slight saline edge. Nice brightness and focus, but has a rich mid-palate. 95/100

Prophet’s Rock Antipodes 2022 Central Otago, New Zealand
The original collaboration wine with François Millet. Pinot Noir, made his way. So textural and fine with nice precision to the fruit and a saline twist on the finish. Nice density and structure with a purity and elegance, too. This is brilliant. 95/100

Prophet’s Rock Retrospect 2019 Central Otago, New Zealand
Nice depth here with a stony edge and some iodine notes. Grippy and textural with layers of flavour. Stony and mineral, this is developing nicely. 95/100

title: Smart organic wines from A&D Wines, Vinho Verde, Portugal
summary: Website: https://andwines.pt/en/ This is a project based in the Baião subregion of Vinho Verde right next to the Douro. It
content:
Website: https://andwines.pt/en/

This is a project based in the Baião subregion of Vinho Verde right next to the Douro. It began with a family quinta Casa do Arrabalde (5 hectares of vines), and another property Quinta dos Espinhosos (7 hectares of vines) and this was then supplemented with the purchase of the much larger (33 hectares) Quinta de Santa Teresa.

They now have 45 hectares of vines all organically certified. Oenotourism is important here. All ferments are with indigenous yeasts.

Casa do Arrabalde 2024 Vinho Verde, Portugal
A field blend of Avesso, Alvarinho and Arinto from vineyards at 550 m. Fresh and supple with nice weight, and a strong mineral edge to the textured citrus fruit. Lively. 92/100

Quinta de Santa Teresa Monólogo Santa Teresa Avesso 2024 Vinho Verde, Portugal
Fresh, bright and linear with lovely weight and texture. Has direct citrus and pear fruit, and a nice crystalline quality. Very stylish. 93/100

Quinta de Santa Teresa Monólogo Santa Teresa Arinto 2024 Vinho Verde, Portugal
Fresh, bright and quite linear with lovely purity. Slightly salty lemony directness but also some nice texture. Lovely wine. 93/100

Quinta de Santa Teresa Singular 2024 Vinho Verde, Portugal
This is a blend of Avesso, Arinto, Alvarinho and Malvasia Fina. 10% in oak. So textural and lively with bright lemony fruit and some ripe pear. Fresh and linear with a nice acid line. Pure with a wide dynamic range. 93/100

Quinta de Santa Teresa Avesso 2024 Vinho Verde, Portugal
Nice density with bright, pure citrus fruit. Good concentration, showing mineral-laced citrus and nice saline detail. Really expressive. 94/100

Quinta de Santa Teresa Esculpido Avesso Vinhas Velhas 2023 Vinho Verde, Portugal
13% alcohol. Old vines, 20% new oak. Powerful and layered with some spicy oak supporting the lively lemony fruit. Has freshness and concentration, with the oak adding an extra dimension. 92/100

Quinta de Santa Teresa Rosé 2024 Vinho Verde, Portugal
This is 90% Touriga Nacional, 10% Vinhão. Textured and layered with sappy red cherry fruit and some strawberry. Bright but with good texture. Stylish. 92/100

Quinta de Santa Teresa Avesso Curtimenta 2023 Vinho Verde, Portugal
Fermented on skins for two weeks. Bight, lively and expressive with citrus, peach and some zippy acidity. Good structure and nice brightness, showing lovely energy. 93/100

UK agent: Graft Wines

title: The wines of Son Antem Alaró, Mallorca, Spain (updated with new releases)
summary: Website: https://www.sonantemalaro.es/en/ Mallorca isn’t well known as a wine island, but there are some interesting projects here, and a recent
content:
Website: https://www.sonantemalaro.es/en/

Mallorca isn’t well known as a wine island, but there are some interesting projects here, and a recent addition to the fine wine scene here is the Son Antem project, which was started by a wine-importing couple in Sweden, Johan and Marin Lidby. I tried through the range and came away impressed.

Son Antem Alaró has 18 hectares of vines, all hand managed by the Lidby family.

Son Antem Malva 2024 Vino de la Tierra Mallorca, Spain
14% alcohol. This white is powerful, rich and intense with a lively acid line lying under the rich pear and peach fruit. The limestone gives the bracing acidity that provides a nice foil for the bold, slightly pithy fruit. Such nice intensity to this distinctive white wine, with a lively acid character driving things. 93/100

Son Antem Aura 2024 Vino de la Tierra Mallorca, Spain
13% alcohol. This white is made from the Giró Rus variety. Bold, rich and textural with some honey, nut and spice characters as well as some pear and peach. There’s innate richness, but it stays fresh and crystalline, with a touch of aniseed and some grainy detail. 92/100

Camins de Son Antem Callet 2024 Vino de la Tierra Mallorca, Spain
12.5% alcohol. This is a lighter-coloured red wine with beautiful aromatics of sweet strawberries and some glace cherry. In the mouth it is very textural with white pepper, cherry, strawberry and fine spicy notes. Lovely fine-grained texture here. Sleek and sweetly fruited with finesse. 94/100

Son Antem S’Era 2024 Vino de la Tierra Mallorca, Spain
12.5% alcohol. Relatively pale in colour, this is is juicy, fine and delicate. There’s a rich mid-palate core of sweet cherry and plum fruit with some damson bitterness, and also some dried herb character. The variety here is Callet, and it seems brilliantly adapted to this warm climate, with some rose, cherry, aniseed and mediterranean herb notes. It’s a little compressed on the finish, with some tannin and acid providing structure, but overall this is really compelling. 94/100

Camins de Son Antem Negre 2024 Vino de la Tierra Mallorca, Spain
14% alcohol. Manto Negro, Monastrell and Syrah. This is sleek, ripe and textured with appealing black cherry and berry fruits. There’s an innate richness and ripeness, but it’s hemmed in by a bit of grainy structure and just enough acidity. And the fruit character stays deliciously floral and fresh. 93/100

Older notes:

Son Antem Camins de Mallorca Giro Blanco 2023 Mallorca, Spain
12.5% alcohol. This is concentrated and grainy and salty with sweet, textured, ripe pear and citrus fruit (a touch of mandarin and lemon). This has such a lovely mineral flow on the palate with real finesse. Very expressive: ripe but well defined. 94/100

Son Antem Malva 2023 Mallorca
14% alcohol. This is really rich and expansive with sweet pear and table grape fruitiness, as well as some spicy highlights and a touch of saltiness. Rich, exuberant and really fruity, this has lots of personality, and good acidity balancing the sweet fruit. 91/100

Son Antem Camins de Mallorca Rouge 2022 Mallorca, Spain
12.5% alcohol. Lovely aromatics here with sleek cherries, raspberries and some aniseed, as well as a hint of pepper. The palate has a sweet fruit core with nice spicy detail. Pure and refined with warm Mediterranean herb notes but always so balanced with real finesse. Grainy and delicious. 93/100

Son Antem Alaró S’Era 2023 IGP Mallorca, Spain
12% alcohol. This is varietal Callet. Quite a pale cherry red colour, in the in-between land, not quite rosé, not quite red wine. Very fine and supple with sweet red cherry fruit and a silky elegance, and just a hint of structure. Has lovely texture and warmth with rounded sweet cherry fruit. 94/100

Son Antem Vespre 2022 Mallorca, Spain
12.5% alcohol. This is supple, expressive and elegant with real elegance, and a sweet, textured mid-palate with stony cherries and plums, and then notes of herbs, pepper and fine spices framing the fruit on the finish. This is very fine: restrained, sappy and showing warm-climate elegance. 94/100

Son Antem Sa Tanca 2022 Mallorca, Spain
13% alcohol. This is Manto Negro, from iron-rich stony soils. Beautiful aromatics: sweet cherry and raspberry fruit with some nice spicy detail and a beautiful flow across the palate. Ripe but really elegance with great purity. 95/100

title: Highlights: Louis Jadot Famille Gagey Bouzeron 2023
summary: Website: https://www.louisjadot.com/bo/wp-content/pdf/vins/vin-1325-bouzeron.pdf Previously labelled as Domaine Gagey, this is now Famille Gagey, presumably because some non-domaine fruit enters the blend.
content:
Website: https://www.louisjadot.com/bo/wp-content/pdf/vins/vin-1325-bouzeron.pdf

Previously labelled as Domaine Gagey, this is now Famille Gagey, presumably because some non-domaine fruit enters the blend. Bouzeron is a small appellation in Bourgogne of 47 hectares, devoted solely to Aligoté. This is a superb example of what this variety can do, and it’s vinified two-thirds in tank and then one-third in barrels, of which 20% are new.

Louis Jadot Famille Gagey Bouzeron 2023 Bourgogne, France
This is a really good Aligoté. Lovely aromatics with lively citrus notes as well as ripe pear. The palate is fine grained and textural with lovely depth of flavour, and notes of pear skin, crystalline lemons and a hint of apricot. So lovely, showing real depth, and a slight saltiness on the finish. 94/100

title: How pruning is done in Bourgogne (Burgundy)
summary: I was recently filming in Bourgogne (Burgundy), and there were lots of gaps while cameras and lighting were being set
content:
I was recently filming in Bourgogne (Burgundy), and there were lots of gaps while cameras and lighting were being set up. So I went to look at the vines. It was early April and the buds had burst and the leaves were coming out. It’s a good time to see the way the vines are pruned. Here are a bunch of pictures showing some of the variation, almost all taken in Grand Cru Corton Charlemagne vineyards.

Viticulture is quite uniform here. Vines are planted at high density, with 10 000 of them per hectare, and 1 x 1 m spacing. The fruiting wire is low at around 30 cm. The vines are single-Guyot pruned, with a short cane of 6-8 buds, and a two bud replacement spur if there’s one to be found. Each vine is only being asked to ripen a small quantity of grapes because of the close spacing. Canopies aren’t high and because of the spacing, over-row tractors are used, stradding the row they are treating.

I saw very little herbicide use: these are expensive vineyards, and almost everyone works the soil. In less fancy vineyards, the situation might be different. Once you leave the more celebrated appellations vineyards can look quite different in terms of spacing and pruning (for example, the Hautes Côtes).

title: Invincible, a new Douro star winery from Marc Kent and Rita Marques
summary: Website: https://invincible.com.pt/ This is the Douro project of Marc Kent and Rita Marques. Marc is well known as the founder
content:
Website: https://invincible.com.pt/

This is the Douro project of Marc Kent and Rita Marques. Marc is well known as the founder and head of Boekenhoutskloof in South Africa, but he’s fallen in love with the Douro and now spends most of his time there. Rita previously made wines under the Conceito label, including a fabulous Bastardo that I was in love with!

Invincible (full name Companhia de Vinhos Invencível) began in 2000, so it’s still early days for this project, and the top wines are only being released now. The winery is based in the Cima Corgo, right in the heart of the Douro.

These wines range from everyday affordable (the Numero Um is around £12 in the UK, and is available in supermarkets) to more ambitious. They also make a Vinho Verde called Natural Mystic, which retails for £16.

Invincible Número Um Branco 2025 Douro, Portugal (sample)
A little bit of Muscat is used here, fermented in amphora. The resy of the wine is in concrete. Bright and stony with lovely pure fruit. Crisp with nice balance, and real potential. 91/100

Invincible Número Dois Branco 2024 Douro, Portugal
Made from older vineyards, this is half concrete and half barrel. Some oak adds an extra dimension. Bright and linear, this is fresh with some nutty, mealy hints, with nice balance. 91/100

Invincible ‘The Invincible’ Branco 2024 Douro, Portugal
This is first release. Juicy and bright, this is a vivid white wine with nice intensity. Zippy with some stony minerality and a touch of spicy oak. Very sophisticated. 93/100

Invincible Número Um Tinto 2024 Douro, Portugal
Juicy, bright and fruity with a nice focus to the sweet raspberry and cherry fruit. Vital. Lovely fruit with liqourice and a touch of pepper. 92/100 (this is £12.75 in Sainsbury’s in the UK.)

Invincible Número Dois Tinto 2023 Douro, Portugal
60 year old vines, fermented in concrete tanks, then spends one year in barrel and another year in concrete tank. Juicy, bright and vivid with nice spicy framing to the fresh cherry and berry fruits, with a lick of liquorice. Modern in style showing bright fruit, with some tarriness around the margins. 92/100

Invincible ‘The Invincible’ Tinto 2023 Douro, Portugal
From a vineyard planted in 1930. Concentrated but supple with lovely pure black cherry and blackberry fruit with a nice salty edge and some good tannic structure. Very fine and expressive. 94/100

Natural Mystic Vinhas Velhas 2025 Vinho Verde, Portugal
Mostly Loureiro. Lively, vivid and bright with nice focus and fruitiness. Very zippy but has texture and balance. 92/100

title: Brindiwell, a new producer in Devon, England
summary: Website: https://www.brindiwell.co.uk/pages/about-us I first met James Matyear when he was managing the vineyard at Black Chalk. But with his wife
content:
Website: https://www.brindiwell.co.uk/pages/about-us

I first met James Matyear when he was managing the vineyard at Black Chalk. But with his wife Jo he’s now got his own project. Back in 2016 they found their site, and after diligent preparation, planted in 2018. Viticulture is organic less the certification, with no herbicides used. They’ve also planted 1000 native trees on the site, including 600 oak trees. This is very cool.

Brindiwell Blanc de Noirs 2022 Devon, England
12.2% alcohol. 26 months on lees, with 9% of the base wine aged in barrel. This is fresh and aromatic with lovely pure cherry and lemon fruit, showing nice delicacy and texture. Fruit-driven with real finesse and purity, and a hint of sherbetty brightness on the finish. The balance here is superb: it’s fruit dominant, but not techno, with some personality. This is the combination of a really good site, good viticulture and sensitive winemaking. And it’s just the first release. 92/100

Brindiwell Pinot Noir Rosé 2023 Devon, England
12.6% alcohol. Clones 114, 115 and 777, stainless steel ferment and ageing, 25% malolactic fermentation. Pale in colour, this is a really fine, delicate, textural rose with sweet red cherry fruit, a touch of redcurrant and raspberry, with some fine sappy, leafy hints in the background. There’s a nice sleek quality to the fruit, and it finishes with a fine crystalline spicy quality. This is really good. 92/100

title: Domaine de Bon Augure, an exciting wine estate in the Languedoc with very interesting soils
summary: Website: https://www.domainedebonaugure.fr/ Cédric Guy farms 9 hectares of very interesting vineyards near the village of Joncels, with distinctive soils. They
content:
Website: https://www.domainedebonaugure.fr/

Cédric Guy farms 9 hectares of very interesting vineyards near the village of Joncels, with distinctive soils. They are ‘breche calcaire’, which translates as brecchiated limestone. Small limestone pebbles are cemented together, and this is really distinctive.

The wines are currently labelled IGP Haute Vallée de l’Orb, but soon they may be part of a new AOP called Monts d’Avene, which will be restricted to Chardonnay on the brecchiated limestone. Bon Augure has 6 hectares of Chardonnay, and also some Petit Manseng, Petite Arvine (sur echalas), Sauvignon Gris, Grenache Gris and Pinot Gris.

Farming is organic/biodynamic, and the altitude here is 600 m.

These wines are imported into the UK by Saison Wines.

Domaine de Bon Augure En Terre Étrangère 2024  IGP Haute Vallée de l’Orb, France
13% alcohol. Chardonnay, 50% barrel fermented, used oak. Textured, fresh and linear with lovely weight and real precision. There’s concentration and depth, but it’s not in the least bit fat, with citrus and pear fruit, finishing fresh and focused. 94/100 (UK retail c. £20)

Domaine de Bon Augure Joncs-Cella 2024 IGP Haute Vallée de l’Orb, France
13% alcohol. The name is Latin for Joncels. This is 80% Chardonnay and 20% Petit Manseng, fully barrel fermented in new oak. This is very fine and textural with a spicy framing to the rich pear and citrus fruit. Lovely precision with a fine acid line. Real focus and depth here, and it stays fresh with a nice bitter hint on the finish. 94/100 (UK retail c. £24)

Domaine de Bon Augure Aux Innocents Les Mains Pleines 2024 IGP Haute Vallée de l’Orb, France
This translates to ‘to the innocents, full hands’. It’s a skin-fermented wine with Sauvignon Gris, Grenache Gris and Pinot Gris, all of which have some pigmentation in their skins, so not technically an orange wine. 7 days on skins in stainless steel. Vivid amber colour. Refined, concentrated, powerful. Lovely intensity here with bold fruit and a slight saltiness on the finish. Great acidity, and real sense of refinement. 95/100 (UK retail c. £20)

Domaine de Bon Augure DSLS 2021 IGP Haute Vallée de l’Orb, France
13% alcohol. Petite Arvine that spends 2 years in new barrels, which are from the ‘nuits’ range of Sirugue, a higher quality barrel designed for the most concentrated wines. And it soaks up the oak very well. Amazing vivid spicy nose with crystalline citrus fruit. The palate has spice, citrus and nuts with

title: Highlights: Tenuta San Guido La Difese 2024 Toscana IGT, Italy
summary: This is the bargain in the line-up from Tenuta San Guido: of course, Sassicaia and Guidalberto are amazing, but they
content:
This is the bargain in the line-up from Tenuta San Guido: of course, Sassicaia and Guidalberto are amazing, but they aren’t that accessible, whereas this delivers a lot of the personality at a more affordable price. I really like it.

Tenuta San Guido La Difese 2024 Toscana IGT, Italy
13.5% alcohol. 75% Sangiovese and 25% Cabernet Sauvignon. This is a beautiful expression of Bolgheri that’s bright, linear, juicy and tart with sour cherry and raspberry, as well as amazing presence. There are some nice green hints along with the juicy fruits, and good acidity. A wholesome, fresh, linear wine with good tannins and acidity. Such elegance and drinkability. 93/100 (UK retail is c. £30)

summary: Website: https://www.domainedebonaugure.fr/ Cédric Guy farms 9 hectares of very interesting vineyards near the village of Joncels, with distinctive soils. They

And it soaks up the oak very well. Amazing vivid spicy nose with crystalline citrus fruit. The palate has spice, citrus and nuts with

title: Highlights: Tenuta San Guido La Difese 2024 Toscana IGT, Italy
summary: This is the bargain in the line-up from Tenuta San Guido: of course, Sassicaia and Guidalberto are amazing, but they
content:
This is the bargain in the line-up from Tenuta San Guido: of course, Sassicaia and Guidalberto are amazing, but they aren’t that accessible, whereas this delivers a lot of the personality at a more affordable price. I really like it.

Tenuta San Guido La Difese 2024 Toscana IGT, Italy

There are some nice green hints along with the juicy fruits, and good acidity. A wholesome, fresh, linear wine with good tannins and acidity. Such elegance and drinkability. 93/100 (UK retail is c. £30)

title: The hidden cost of buying wine en primeur, and why it now makes little sense
summary: Buying wine en primeur has two potential benefits. First, if the wine is sought after, you secure your allocation. If
content:
Buying wine en primeur has two potential benefits. First, if the wine is sought after, you secure your allocation. If that’s the case in your situation, then you can ignore the rest of this post. But for most Bordeaux, the wine most widely sold this way, this is rarely the case.

Second, if the release price is attractive, then it can be the cheapest way of buying the wine. The problem here is that in recent years the châteaux have been keen not to let the customers and secondary market take too much of their margin, and have adjusted their prices upwards. Back in the day, canny drinkers would buy two cases, and pay for the consumption of one case by flipping the other at a later date (although, as we’ll see, their economics might not have been all that solid).

But there are some hidden costs that collectors sometimes forget, when they sell wines many years after buying them and are basking in the profits they think they have made.

As an example, let’s say that 15 years ago you bought a case that ended up all-in (duty plus VAT included) at £1200. The current market price, all taxes paid, is now £2400. It looks like you have made quite a profit.

But let’s adjust for inflation.

That £1200 from 2011 is now £1897 in 2026, according to the Bank of England inflation calculator.

And let’s not forget about the storage costs, before the wine is delivered, which work out quite expensive. I looked at some of the prices in the UK.

  • Corney & Barrow £18.60 per 12 bottle case (stored per unit = 6 bottles, insurance included)
  • Berry Bros & Rudd £17.28 per 12 bottle case
  • The Wine Society £10.28 per 12 bottle case (includes insurance)
  • Seckford Wines £13.20 per 12 bottles
  • Big Yellow also offer wine storage in London, but this is based on a certain size of storage unit. You have full access to your wine during opening hours. This is not in bond storage.

Let’s add the charges over 15 years at an average of £14. A conservative figure.
This is £210 (and I should have done some interest calculations on each of the payments, but I didn’t, so this is a low figure), bringing your £1200 up to £2107. Suddenly the profit doesn’t look so much!

But it gets worse if you look at lost opportunity cost.

Imagine you had invested the £1200 in a low-cost global ETF yielding (conservatively) 7% per annum, and then you compound the gains.

The £1200 is now £3310

This means you have made a loss!

Let’s make this practical. What were the actual prices in 2010, which was regarded as a very good vintage at the time, and would have been sold in 2011 en primeur?

As an example, here’s a list of projected prices at the time, in bond per case of 12.

The likes of Montrose and Haut-Bailly were £900

Montrose got some 100 point scores and is now selling for £1650 in bond

Haut-Bailly is selling for £1200 in bond

Using lost opportunity cost without storage costs these wines will have made losses of
£833 and £1283 respectively. And this is for high-performing investment-grade wines from a good vintage.

In general, you are much better going into the secondary market.

title: Domaine de Bon Augure, an exciting wine estate in the Languedoc with very interesting soils
summary: Website: https://www.domainedebonaugure.fr/ Cédric Guy farms 9 hectares of very interesting vineyards near the village of Joncels, with distinctive soils. They
content:
Website: https://www.domainedebonaugure.fr/

Cédric Guy farms 9 hectares of very interesting vineyards near the village of Joncels, with distinctive soils. They are ‘breche calcaire’, which translates as brecchiated limestone. Small limestone pebbles are cemented together, and this is really distinctive.

The wines are currently labelled IGP Haute Vallée de l’Orb, but soon they may be part of a new AOP called Monts d’Avene, which will be restricted to Chardonnay on the brecchiated limestone. Bon Augure has 6 hectares of Chardonnay, and also some Petit Manseng, Petite Arvine (sur echalas), Sauvignon Gris, Grenache Gris and Pinot Gris.

Farming is organic/biodynamic, and the altitude here is 600 m.

These wines are imported into the UK by Saison Wines.

Domaine de Bon Augure En Terre Étrangère 2024  IGP Haute Vallée de l’Orb, France
13% alcohol. Chardonnay, 50% barrel fermented, used oak. Textured, fresh and linear with lovely weight and real precision. There’s concentration and depth, but it’s not in the least bit fat, with citrus and pear fruit, finishing fresh and focused. 94/100 (UK retail c. £20)

Domaine de Bon Augure Joncs-Cella 2024 IGP Haute Vallée de l’Orb, France
13% alcohol. The name is Latin for Joncels. This is 80% Chardonnay and 20% Petit Manseng, fully barrel fermented in new oak. This is very fine and textural with a spicy framing to the rich pear and citrus fruit. Lovely precision with a fine acid line. Real focus and depth here, and it stays fresh with a nice bitter hint on the finish. 94/100 (UK retail c. £24)

Domaine de Bon Augure Aux Innocents Les Mains Pleines 2024 IGP Haute Vallée de l’Orb, France
This translates to ‘to the innocents, full hands’. It’s a skin-fermented wine with Sauvignon Gris, Grenache Gris and Pinot Gris, all of which have some pigmentation in their skins, so not technically an orange wine. 7 days on skins in stainless steel. Vivid amber colour. Refined, concentrated, powerful. Lovely intensity here with bold fruit and a slight saltiness on the finish. Great acidity, and real sense of refinement. 95/100 (UK retail c. £20)

Domaine de Bon Augure DSLS 2021 IGP Haute Vallée de l’Orb, France
13% alcohol. Petite Arvine that spends 2 years in new barrels, which are from the ‘nuits’ range of Sirugue, a higher quality barrel designed for the most concentrated wines. And it soaks up the oak very well. Amazing vivid spicy nose with crystalline citrus fruit. The palate has spice, citrus and nuts with
